coredata.dc:title: Cu, I-doped carbon dots as simulated nanozymes for the colorimetric detection of morphine in biological samples
coredata.dc:creator: Yin, Qinhong
Yang, Xiaolan
Yang, Lihua
Yang, Dezhi
Yang, Yaling
Zhu, Yanqin
coredata.dc:description: As newly developed synthetic enzymes with exceptional catalytic capabilities and outstanding stability, nanozymes have drawn considerable interest in the realm of sensing. Using a simple hydrothermal process, iodine and copper-doped carbon dots (Cu,I-CDs) with simulated enzymes were fabricated in the current investigation. Cu,I-CDs demonstrate peroxidase-mimicking function together with high catalytic effectiveness due to aforementioned features. This led to generation of a colorimetric sensor for quick and accurate quantitative assessment of morphine (MOR). The outcomes showed the method's usefulness for the colorimetric detection of MOR. The linear range for MOR detection is 0.25–25 μg/mL having a reduced detection limit of 64 ng/mL. This sensor's successful use in the analysis of MOR in biological material is more noteworthy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Copper
Iodine
Carbon dots
Nanozymes
Morphine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of real samples and standard solutions
Colorimetric detection of MOR with Cu,I-CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic illustrating the synthesis and morphine detection of Cu, I-CDs.
The analysis of Cu,I-CDs using (A) TEM images, (B) Size distribution histograms obtained from TEM images, (C)FT-IR and (D) XRD.
(A) XPS spectra of Cu,I-CDs, high resolution (B) C 1s, (C) N 1s, (D) O 1s, (E) I 3d and (F) Cu 2p peaks of Cu, I-CDs.
Michaelis-Menten curves and Lineweaver-Burk double-reciprocal diagram of Cu,I-CDs with (A) TMB and (B) H2O2 as substrates.

(A) Comparison of absorbance values after adding ascorbic acid. (B) Fluorescence spectra after adding PTA. (C) EPR spectra of the DMPO/•OH spin adduct of Cu,I-CDs/H2O2. (D) The UV spectra of the Cu,I-CDs + H2O2+ TMB system with the presence of different concentrations of MOR.
Effect of (A) time, (B) Cu,I-CDs concentration, (C) pH and (D) temperature.
Effect of (A) interfering ions and (B) interfering substances. Stability of Cu,I-CDs at different pH (C) and temperature (D).
The linear relationship between the absorbance and MOR concentration in the range of 0.25–25 μg/mL.
Steady-state kinetic parameters for Cu,I-CDs and other oxidase mimics.
The overview of analytical data of the reported methods for the analysis of MOR.

head.ce:title: Cu, I-doped carbon dots as simulated nanozymes for the colorimetric detection of morphine in biological samples

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As newly developed synthetic enzymes with exceptional catalytic capabilities and outstanding stability, nanozymes have drawn considerable interest in the realm of sensing. Using a simple hydrothermal process, iodine and copper-doped carbon dots (Cu,I-CDs) with simulated enzymes were fabricated in the current investigation. Cu,I-CDs demonstrate peroxidase-mimicking function together with high catalytic effectiveness due to aforementioned features. This led to generation of a colorimetric sensor for quick and accurate quantitative assessment of morphine (MOR). The outcomes showed the method's usefulness for the colorimetric detection of MOR. The linear range for MOR detection is 0.25–25 μg/mL having a reduced detection limit of 64 ng/mL. This sensor's successful use in the analysis of MOR in biological material is more noteworthy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Opioids are a class of drugs, with an extended history, the most widespread circulation, and the cause most serious harm to the population [1]. Morphine is the principal alkaloid obtained from the poppy plant [2]. Since this directly affects the central nervous system and replicates functions of endorphins, endogenous neuropeptides with potent analgesic/euphoric performance, morphine reflects a highly effective medication in clinical settings in treating severe pain [3]. As a metabolite of lternative opioids, morphine is regarded as an illicit drug as well. It has potentially dangerous side effects, such as low blood pressure and slow breathing, and it also serves as a sign that other opioids are being abused [4]. On the other hand, heroin, which is morphine's major metabolite, is the main precursor to the manufacture of several other opioid drugs [5]. Analysis of morphine is necessary for the medical and forensic fields to keep track of the levels of MOR in biological specimens and to avoid toxicities brought on by addiction or overdose.

The traditional methods for on-spot rapid detection of illicit drugs mainly include the chemical method [6], the test paper method (immunocolloidal gold technology) [7], and the portable spectral detection method [8,9]. These methods are fast and convenient in terms of operation, however, they suffer from disadvantages such as low specificity and sensitivity. Therefore, the analyzed sample also needs to be sent to a specialized laboratory for analysis and confirmation by precision instruments such as GC-MS [10,11] and HPLC-MS [12,13]. Of course, due to their size and the need for trained individuals to utilize them, it is rather impossible to employ such equipment for quick identification for front-line law-enforcing.

Owing to their great stability, cheap cost, and convenience of mass manufacture, nanozymes, a group of nanomaterials having natural enzyme-like function, were employed as biosensors, capable of replacing natural enzymes [14–17]. Many nanomaterials which mimic enzyme activity, have been explored to date, which include Q-graphene [18], V2O5 nanobelts [19], metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) [20], noble metal nanoparticles [21], and alloy nanoparticles [22]. Such artificial enzymes fabricated from inorganic nanomaterials can successfully bypass the inherent limits of natural enzymes due to their simple manufacturing and variable catalytic activity. They have thus found extensive use in the fields of biomedicine and sensing [23–26].

Currently, Nanozymes used for analysis and detection include transition metal oxides (such as ferromagnetic nanoparticles) [27], non-metallic nanomaterials (such as silicon dots) [28], transition metal sulfides (WS2 nanosheets) [29], etc. However, these nanozymes materials also have some shortcomings such as poor water solubility and the requirement for rare metals [30]. Because of their outstanding qualities, including being highly stable, with elevated aqueous solubility, reduced toxicity, together with good biocompatibility, nanozymes doped with different elements have been extensively employed in a variety of industries [31–33]. Additionally, the chemical valence of metal elements can be changed using the charge separation, transfer, and reduction capabilities of CDs [34,35]. Thus, it is envisaged that metal-based nanozymes will be fabricated using CDs that resemble enzyme action.

In this study, hydrothermal synthesis is employed for the fabrication of nanostructures based on iodine and copper-doped carbon dots (Cu,I-CDs). Following confirmation that Cu,I-CDs nanozyme alone exhibits peroxidase-like function, steady-state kinetics are methodically examined, while functions of radicals within Cu,I-CDs nanozyme catalysis are investigated. In addition, the linear relationship between MOR concentration and the colorimetric reaction absorbance was determined. To detect MOR quickly and precisely, a fast colorimetric sensing method based on the Cu,I-CDs nanozyme is proposed. To the best of the authors’ knowledge, Cu,I-CDs nanozyme biosensor is the first of its type and has a high sensitivity and selectivity for colorimetric detecting MOR as distinct molecules in 5 min.

Copper chloride (CuCl2·2H2O), 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), and 3-iodo- l -tyrosine were purchased from the Aladdin Industrial Corporation. H2O2 (30%) was procured from the Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Factory. Ferric trichloride (FeCl3·6H2O) was purchased from Zhiyuan Chemical Factory (Tianjin, China). The MOR standard was provided by the Ministry of Public Security's Key Laboratory of Narcotics Assay and Control Technology. All remaining chemicals were acquired through Fengchuan Chemical Reagent Technologies (Tianjin, China) and Zhiyuan Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. Doubly-distilled water obtained via Milli-Q® filtration platform (Millipore™, USA), serving as input for furnishing deionized water.

Colorimetric detection of MOR was performed through a Shimadzu™ UV-2550® spectrophotometer (Shimadzu™, Japan) using a quartz cuvette with a 10 mm optical path. The FT-IR spectrum was recorded through the TENSOR-27® FTIR spectrometer (Bruker®, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed through PHI5000 Versa Probe-II® (ULVAC-PHI™, Japan) having monochromatized Al K radiation. A TecnaiG2 F30® S-Twin (FEI™, USA) transmission electron microscope, was employed for analysis of the morphology and size of carbon dots. The pH was determined using a Leici PHS-3® digital pH meter (Shanghai, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of real samples and standard solutions
ce:section.ce:para: In the current work, real specimens of urine were obtained from healthy volunteers. Firstly, 80 μL NaOH solution (5%, W/V) was added to a 5 mL urine sample to adjust the pH value to a strong alkaline level (pH > 11). Subsequently, solid NaCl was added until the mixed solution is saturated. Finally, 80 μL cyclohexane as extraction solvent was added and fully mixed. After centrifugation, the upper organic layer was taken for reserve. A stock-standard solution of MOR (100 μg/mL) in water was made. To create working solutions, deionized water was used to dilute the stock standard solutions. All solutions were stored at 4 °C in the refrigerator.

Fig. 1 displays the flowchart representation for synthesis of the Cu,I-CDs. First, 30 mL of deionized water was used to dissolve 0.1 g of CuCl2, 0.4 g of 3-iodo-l-tyrosine, and 100 μL of ethylenediamine. This combination was then placed in the stainless steel reactor with a PTFE liner and heated for 8 h at 180 °C. The obtained Cu,I-CDs solution was centrifuged (15 min/12,000 rpm) thus removing solid particles, and subsequently acclimatized to ambient/room temperature (RT). Cu,I-CDs were then stored within a refrigerator at 4 °C for later usage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric detection of MOR with Cu,I-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Cu,I-CDs solution (80 μL) having different MOR concentrations, 20 μL of 5 mM FeCl3 solutions, and 80 μL of 50 mM H2O2 solution were mixed (RT). The aforementioned combination was consequently introduced within 100 μL TMB and 1 mL sodium acetate buffer (pH = 4). This reaction was conducted for 5 min at 27 °C. The MOR concentrations were 0.25, 1.25, 2.5, 7.5, 12.5, 22.5, and 25 μg/mL. Finally, this system was analyzed for its absorbance (A) at 654 nm. MOR selectivity by such sensing platform was assessed within presence of Na+, NO2 −, Fe2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, K+, Ca2+, and other interfering substances, such as glycine, l-cysteine, tryptophan, glutamic, glucose.

Nanostructural features for Cu,I-CDs were analyzed through TEM. Cu,I-CDs TEM imaging (multiple scales) are depicted within Fig. 2 A and size distribution histograms obtained in Fig. 2B. A wide range of particle sizes exist among the synthesized carbon dots: with the smallest ones being 2 nm in size, while the largest ones were close to 6 nm as seen in Fig. 2A. The majority of Cu, I-CDs have a spherical shape with homogenous distribution. At the same time, The HRTEM image shows obvious lattice fringes with the distinct lattice 0.22 nm, corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of graphitic carbon in the inset of Fig. 2A, indicating a good degree of crystallinity in carbon dots, which is consistent with previous studies [36,37].

In Fig. 2C, the FT-IR spectra of Cu,I-CDs have been displayed. The interlayer hydroxyl group's N–H stretching vibration is responsible for the absorption band near 3414 cm−1 in the infrared spectrum. Peaks at 2059 cm−1 related towards stretching and bending vibrations for C–H [38]. Peaks at 1618 cm−1 and 1403 cm−1, respectively, were attributable towards CO and C–N bending vibrations. Peaks at 1605 cm−1 and 1061 cm−1, respectively, may be linked towards C–O and CC bending vibrations [39]. These outcomes validated the presence of –NH2, C–N, and CO groups, that improved Cu,I-CDs' water solubility.

Next, powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) was used for assessing crystallinity by Cu,I-CDs, and the results are presented in Fig. 2D. Cu,I-CDs' XRD pattern showed typical 2θ peaks appear at 22°, this pattern indicates the arrangement of carbon lattice in HRTEM image [37].

Chemical groups upon Cu,I-CDs surfaces were studied using XPS. Peaks in the spectrum at 284.5, 401.2, 531.6, 618.3 and 931.2 eV were caused by concentrations of C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, I 3d and Cu 2p (Fig. 3 A–D) [40]. Further investigation of the I 3d spectra reveals two prominent peaks from the I 3d3/2 and I 3d5/2 groups at 618.3 and 629.7 eV (Fig. 3E) [37]. Cu 2p spectrum has a peak that could relate to Cu+ at 931.2 eV (2p3/2) (Fig. 3F) [41]. The presence of variable valence Cu and I in Cu,I-CDs enhanced the nanozymes activity.

Kinetic variables of nanozymes were examined to comprehend peroxidase-mimicking function by Cu,I-CDs within greater detail (Fig. 4 A and B). As a result, reaction velocity vs. substrate concentration curves was obtained, which are consistent with the Michaelis-Menten equation. Additionally, the double reciprocal Lineweaver-Burk diagram was used to calculate the catalytic kinetic parameters (K
m and V
max ) of H2O2 and TMB. The findings demonstrated that when H2O2 was used as the substrate, Cu,I-CDs had a Km value that was 1.74 times higher than HRP's and a V
max value that was 8.77 times higher. When using TMB as the substrate, the K
m value was similar to HRP's, and the V
max value was 1.82 times greater [42]. Compared with the results reported in the literature [42–45], it is found that Cu,I-CDs have better affinity for substrates and higher maximum reaction rate than other oxidase mimics (Table 1 ), and Cu,I-CDs are characterized by simple raw materials and convenient synthesis.

The generation of reactive oxygen species ROS, typically deemed as generated within reactions catalyzed by nano-enzymes was investigated, for additional confirmation of catalytic mechanism by Cu,I-CDs nanozyme. It is well known that ascorbic acid (AA) can act as a reducing agent, and is capable of being oxidized into dehydroascorbic acid by reactive oxygen species (ROS) generated by Cu,I-CDs [46]. As a result, the peak of AA + Cu,I-CDs disappears at 266 nm when AA is merely added to the reaction system under the same test circumstances, and the absorbance of the system with AA + Cu,I-CDs + MOR is lower in comparison to that of the AA + Cu,I-CDs system (Fig. 5 A). This finding suggests that ROS generation is responsible for the catalytic reaction of H2O2 by Cu,I-CDs.

According to earlier studies, superoxide anion (•O2 −) and hydroxide radicals (•OH) are often the intermediate active species when nanozyme catalyze H2O2 [41]. p-phthalic acid (PTA) was chosen as a fluorescent probe to follow •OH generation within reaction, confirming catalytic mechanism of Cu,I-CDs. Since PTA has a low level of fluorescence, •OH can combine with PTA to create hydroxybenzoic acid, which has a high level of fluorescence. According to Fig. 5B, the inclusion of Cu,I-CDs and PTA results in an apparent rise in fluorescence intensity, suggesting the generation of •OH. The fluorescence signal is greatly reduced when Cu,I-CDs, PTA, and MOR are present together. The aforementioned findings demonstrate that Cu,I-CDs' catalytic mechanism as a peroxidase mimic is attributable to •OH generation.

Additionally, the EPR spectrum can serve for observing •OH generation. The product in the DMPO + H2O2+ Cu,I-CDs system has four distinct strong peaks, depicted within Fig. 5C, with intensity ratio for EPR spectrum being 1:2:2:1, further validating this finding [41]. Because of this, it may be hypothesized that the mechanism regulating MOR colorimetric detection employing Cu,I-CDs may involve catalytic breakdown of H2O2 through Cu,I-CDs peroxidase-mimicking activity for developing •OH initially, subsequently oxidizing chromogenic substrate TMB, with such oxidation product oxTMB having intense absorption peak at 654 nm, with the introduction of MOR into the system resulted in the reduction of blue oxTMB to colorless TMB, thereby leading to a noticeable decline in the absorbance at 654 nm. UV spectra for Cu,I-CDs + H2O2+ TMB system with various MOR concentrations have been shown in Fig. 5D. With the presence of MOR, a distinct color shift from blue to colorless was seen, providing a strong foundation for MOR detection.

The main detection parameters, such as the irradiation time, the reaction pH, TMB concentration, and Cu,I-CDs dosage, were tuned in an attempt to achieve highly sensitive colorimetric monitoring for MOR depending upon peroxidase-mimicking function by Cu,I-CDs. According to the findings, as depicted in Fig. 6 A, the absorbance signal first underwent an increase with the increase in the irradiation period, before leveling off when the irradiation time increased passed 5 min. Up until a dose of 0.04 mg/mL of Cu,I-CDs, the signal change increased, and additional increases in Cu,I-CDs dosage did not affect the signal (Fig. 6B). According to Fig. 6C, Cu,I-CDs displayed the best oxidase-like activity at pH 4.0, and the same pH level also produced the highest signal difference. When the temperature rose to 27 °C at the same time, a similar pattern was seen (Fig. 6D), with consequent absorbance being reduced once temperature rose >27 °C. The effects of different ionic strength by varying the NaCl concentration on the absorbance were also detected. The results displayed that even if the ion concentration is as high as 400 μM, the absorbance of the Cu,I-CDs + TMB + H2O2 system emerged minor changes. As a result, the ideal experimental parameters for MOR detection were adopted: a Cu,I-CDs concentration of 0.04 mg/mL, a reaction pH of 4.0, a temperature equal to 27 °C, and a detection duration of 5 min.

To validate the selectivity for proposed colorimetric MOR sensing platform, the developed assay was assessed against other interfering ions and substances. The effects of different ions (Na+, NO2 −, Fe2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, K+, Ca2+) and other interfering substances (glycine, l-cysteine, tryptophan, glutamic, glucose) added as 100 μg/mL upon absorbance of MOR on Cu,I-CDs was investigated. The system was found to remain unaffected by these different ions and interfering substances (Fig. 7 A and B). Therefore, the above results confirm the excellent specificity of the proposed detection with Cu,I-CDs nanozymes toward MOR. So that evaluate the stability of Cu,I-CDs, the Cu,I-CDs powder was dispersed in water and preserve at different pH and temperature (Fig. 7C and D). The results showed that Cu,I-CDs still maintained exceeded 90% of the initial catalytic activity after 5 days of store. The above experimental result indicates that Cu,I-CDs has good stability.

By recording the absorbance following the addition of various MOR concentrations under ideal circumstances, the analytical performance for MOR detection was examined. Under ideal circumstances, MOR concentration showed consistency in its pattern as a function of A654nm within 0.25–25 μg/mL spectrum (A = 1.26–0.04C, R2 = 0.9910) (Fig. 8 ). LOD for this method was calculated at 64 ng/mL using the formula 3 s/K. Relative standard deviation (RSD) was 3.8% (C = 1.25 μg/mL, n = 8). This method had a greater selectivity and a detection limit similar to previously recorded MOR probes employing CDs [31–35].

This study used an eco-friendly method to successfully synthesize Cu,I-CDs nanozymes, which manifested a good peroxidase-mimicking activity. The pH, temperature, time, and concentrations of Cu,I-CDs were all optimized for an appropriate level of activity. To measure MOR in actual urine samples, a straightforward and precise colorimetric sensor depending upon peroxidase mimicking activity of Cu,I-CDs was fabricated. This analytical method's development offers a relatively new concept for nanozyme based catalytic detection in colorimetric detection systems and may have future application possibilities in forensic science and drug monitoring.

Qinhong Yin: Characterization, Data analysis, Writing. Xiaolan Yang: Synthesis, Selectivity sensitivity, Data analysis. Lihua Yang: Data analysis, Formal analysis. Dezhi Yang: Validation, Data analysis. Yaling Yang: Supervision, Project administration. Yanqin Zhu: Editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we have no financial and personal relationships with other people or organizations that can inappropriately influence our work, there is no professional or other personal interest of any nature or kind in any product, service and/or company that could be construed as influencing the position presented in, or the review of, the manuscript entitled.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 822603 37), the Opening Project of Key Laboratory of Ministry of Public Security for Drug Analysis and Anti-Drug Technology (No. 2021ZZ04) and Basic Research Project of Yunnan Province/General Project (No. 202001AT070002).

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: U. Boerner, S. Abbott, R.L. Roe, Metabolism of morphine and heroin in man, Drug Metab. Rev. 4 (1975) 39-73.

Kalra, Fungal endophytes of catharanthus roseus enhance vindoline content by modulating structural and regulatory genes related to terpenoid indole alkaloid biosynthesis, Sci. Rep. 6 (2016), 26583.

Sci. 363, (2016) 490-498.
